characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: The show's main protagonist (in theory, at least) he starts the show as an extremely idealistic and innocent boy who just can't fully grasp the extent of his growing pains. An ardent Spielberg fan, he tends to perceive everything around him through a cinematographic lens; his inability tochoose between childhood best friend Joey and mysterious newcomer Jen, and his progressive and slow ambition to become a movie director/screenwriter are the recurring plots of his story on the show. Season 2 sees Dawson dating Joey, although that doesn't last long; Season 3 however sets up his twomost recurring plots: his disillusionment with filmmaking after getting some reality bites, and hisRelationship Revolving Doorwith Joeyand Pacey. He starts a relationship with Pacey's sister Gretchen, and moves to California in Season 5 in order to attend Film Studies at (and drop-out of) South California University. During Season 6, he's on an internship under Todd Carr, until he decides to fund his own film project. Along the way, he films several projects, normallyOnce a Season: aThe Shape of Waterlike retell ofCreature from the Black Lagoon, which is pretty succesful, a retell of his and Joey's relationship, a documentary onCapeside's Witch's Island, a documentary on legendary filmmaker A. I. Brooks, an University project with his friend Oliver Chirchik and, on the final season, a retell of his own teenage years. Those others are... not as succesful. TheGrand Finaleof the show has him as a creator/writer/producer ofThe Creek, a TV series based on his film that's basicallythe in-universe version of Dawson's (the series) first season. The All-American Boy: Dawson is the classic clean-cut all-American nice guy.

characters.name: Dawson Leery
characters.description: The show's main protagonist (in theory, at least) he starts the show as an extremely idealistic and innocent boy who just can't fully grasp the extent of his growing pains. An ardent Spielberg fan, he tends to perceive everything around him through a cinematographic lens; his inability tochoose between childhood best friend Joey and mysterious newcomer Jen, and his progressive and slow ambition to become a movie director/screenwriter are the recurring plots of his story on the show. Season 2 sees Dawson dating Joey, although that doesn't last long; Season 3 however sets up his twomost recurring plots: his disillusionment with filmmaking after getting some reality bites, and hisRelationship Revolving Doorwith Joeyand Pacey. He starts a relationship with Pacey's sister Gretchen, and moves to California in Season 5 in order to attend Film Studies at (and drop-out of) South California University. During Season 6, he's on an internship under Todd Carr, until he decides to fund his own film project. Along the way, he films several projects, normallyOnce a Season: aThe Shape of Waterlike retell ofCreature from the Black Lagoon, which is pretty succesful, a retell of his and Joey's relationship, a documentary onCapeside's Witch's Island, a documentary on legendary filmmaker A. I. Brooks, an University project with his friend Oliver Chirchik and, on the final season, a retell of his own teenage years. Those others are... not as succesful. TheGrand Finaleof the show has him as a creator/writer/producer ofThe Creek, a TV series based on his film that's basicallythe in-universe version of Dawson's (the series) first season. The All-American Boy: Dawson is the classic clean-cut all-American nice guy.

characters.name: Josephine "Joey" Lillian Potter
characters.description: Initially,the only female in the trioshe formed with Pacey and Dawson, Joey is somewhat of a town's pariah at the start of the show, sinceher mother died of cancer, her father abandoned her and her sister and is on prison, and she is on charge of trying to meet ends along with her pregnant sister Bessie and her brother-in-law by working on The Ice House, a family business. The more aloof, mature and cynical counterpart to Dawson, she starts the show as a snarky and abrasive young girl who is nonetheless head-over-heels for Dawson, her best friend since childhood. Over time, however, Joey becomes thede factoprotagonist of the show, becoming more open and sociable and developing an interest in art which, coupled with her excellent academic PA, allows her to free herself from her rather humble upbringing. She dates Dawson in Season 2, although that doesn't last long ─even if they both insistthey are soulmates. In Season 3, she helps Bessie set up a Bed & Breakfast at their own home and, inadvertedly, falls in love withPacey; they would date for almost the entirety of Season 4, although that, too,doesn't last long. She gets an scolarship and moves to Boston in Season 5 in order to attend Literature at Worthington College, where she meets and begrudginly befriendsAudrey Liddell. Season 6 sees herRelationship Revolving Doorwith Eddie Dolingand Dawson and Pacey. TheGrand Finaleof the show has her as a sucessful book publisher in New York. Aloof Dark-Haired Girl: Joey is tall and brunette. She is also calm, cool, composed and aloof in nature. Always Second Best: Felt this way about Jen in season 1, because Jen is seen more desirable by boys, especially Dawson. Amicable Exes: With Dawson. Even though they both admitted that they were each other's soulmate, they still remained close friends.

characters.name: Jennifer "Jen" Lindley
characters.description: The mysterious newcomer to Capeside on the very first episode of the show, Jen isa new-yorkerteen who's came to Capeside in order to take care of her sick grandad. The moment he sees her, she becomes Dawson's dream-girl and a permanent fixture on the lives of him, Joey and Pacey. It's eventually revealed, however, Jen's not as pure and good as Dawson wants to believe, and was in fact sent to Capeside by her parents in order to calm down on hersexually charged,self-destructive behavioron New York. Over the show she will start searching herself (though not always on the most appropriate places) and will become a really trusted and appreciated friend to the main trio. On Season 2, after her grandad passes away and she's shunned off of Dawson's group, Jen starts going back to herHard-Drinking Party Girlways with Abby Morgan ─and gets depressed after her friend'sCruel and Unusual Death.She gets better, though, and is pursued in Season 3 by Henry Parker (though that doesn't last long). In Season 4, she goes to a therapist in order to confront all her issues with her family and friends ─thoughthat doesn't last too long,either. Jen's however convinced to join her best friend Jack at Boston University, where they both enroll in Season 5 (to study what? it's not clear). Season 6 shows her whirlwind romance with C.J. Braxton and...not much else. TheGrand Finaleof the show tells us she went back to Capeside, got pregnant and had a daughter, Amy, to an absent father.Also, that she's dying of a uncurable heart condition. Her evolving relationship with hergrandmais the only recurring plot through all seasons of the show. Anyone Can Die:Jen dies by succumbing to a fatal heart condition after she collapses in the series finale. Always Someone Better: Joey feels this way about Jen in season 1.

characters.name: Pacey John Witter
characters.description: Dawson's best friend and Capeside's resident loser, Pacey is actually the extraverted, charismatic and fun-loving young of the trio. The youngest (and apparently less loved) son of a very big family, even though he's pretty well liked by his friends, Pacey has a really low self-esteem, and is failing high school at the start of the show. During the first season, he's mainly defined by hisaffairwith Capeside's new English Literature teacher, Tamara Jacobs. Halfway the season, however, that ends, and the funny and mature character that is Pacey gets revealed to the audience. Season 2 sees her get an age-appropiate romance with AndieMcPhee(after some good 90'sBelligerent Sexual Tension), and she inspires him to get good grades and become less of a screw-up. That doesn't last long, though, when they break up in Season 3 and he starts hismain plot for the rest of the series: his enemies-to-friends-to-lovers romance to Joey Potter ─while he gets more and more detached form his abusive family. They're together until Season 4, since he doesn't make the transition to college and, instead, goes sailing for an entire summer. In Season 5, he joins a prestigious Boston restaurant as a kitchen assistant thanks to his brother Doug. Season 6 has him inexplicably quit and start a very promising career as a stockbrocker, becoming isolated to her girlfriend Audrey and his friends ─specially, Dawson. They reconcile just in time for theGrand Finale; after theTime Skip, Pacey owns and runs the very succesfully reopened [IceHouse] of Capeside. Abusive Parents: He's considered a loser by his parents and they both treat him horribly, especially his father. Amicable Exes: With Andie. Despite their relationship failed, they friends with each other. Aw, Look! They Really Do Love Each Other: With his father, surprisingly. Most of the time, his father is aJerkasswho hates him but they share a few heartwarming scenes in a couple of episodes.

characters.name: JackMcPhee
characters.description: Because of his sexuality, he's thrown out by his and Andie's father, being welcomed by Jen and his grandma at the end of Season 2. Season 3 sees him trying to reconcile with both his father and his sexuality, and he starts semi-dating Ethan Brady; he also joins Capeside's high-school football team, although he later prefers to be a coach for children. In Season 4, he starts completely dating Tobey Barrett, and in Season 5, he convinces Jen to move to Boston in order to attend college there (to study wathever, it's never stated). He joins afrat, which is the last place he faces homophobia. Season 6 has him being more open and comfortable with his sexuality, and he befriends and briefly dates David "No-Last-Name-Given", while he starts sharing an apartment with Pacey.And not much else. TheGrand Finaleof the show has him back at Capeside, being an inspiring High School teacher and secretely dating Doug, Pacey's older brother. On the last scenes of the show, they both adoptAmy, Jen's daughter. Him and his sister Andie joined the cast (and quickly befriended Dawson's circle) on Season 2 as recurring; he would get promoted to main the following season and for the rest of the show. Characterization Marches On: On Season 6. Jack is generally very reserved and shy about his orientation and his boyfriends (he doesn't recognize Tobey as his boyfriend to his frat brothers in Season 5, for example). That's a far cry from S6's Jack, where he has no problem inviting several partners into his and Jen's house and gets a serious boyfriend in David. His clothing and haircut become more relaxed and sporty as a reflection of this. Out of the main gang, his the one who shows more growth over the years.This is emphasized in episode 6x14, where Joey is pretty surprised of Jack's change from his S2's dorky and shy persona. This is emphasized in episode 6x14, where Joey is pretty surprised of Jack's change from his S2's dorky and shy persona.

characters.name: Andrea "Andie"McPhee
characters.description: Andie has the rare distinction of being the most open and sincere character of the cast,at least at first, and quickly starts a romantic relationship with Pacey early on in her debuting season. Come next season, however, and she hascheatedon Pacey during her stay at a sanitarium. From then on, she would behave erratically until she decides to stealexthasispills from Jen's and collapses at a rave.Her father convinces her to leave for Italyin order to recover and live a more peaceful year until her start at college, where she succesfully studies medicine. She and Jack entered the cast (and quickly befriended Dawson's circle) in Season 2 as recurring, and she got promoted to main in Seasons 3 and 4. Though she left the show in Season 4, she was a guest for the finale... in a deleted scene. Amicable Exes: With Pacy. Despite their relationship failed, they friends with each other. Back for the Finale: In a deleted scene. The Bus Came Back: At the end of Season 4 to graduate from Capeside High. This is her last appearance on the show.

characters.name: Audrey Liddell
characters.description: Ascended Extra: In Season 5, to the point that she's added to the opening credits the following season. Beneath the Mask: After her break up with Pacey in Season 6, the mask cracks up and shows how damaged she really is and how little self-esteem she has. Daddy Issues: As does all the cast, really. She has a conflictive relationship to her parents; her mother shows up early on her tenure on her show, and constantly demeans Audrey, explaining why, as a reaction, she doesn't care what others think of her. Derailing Love Interests: Like Andie, she's another girlfriend of Pacey who starts to derail when the writers want him to get close to Joey. In Season 5, she is portrayed as the first girl to be Pacey's equal, until she suddenly turns from a lovable party girl into a screwed-up alcoholic who ends up in rehab the following season. Dumb Blonde: Subverted. She doesn't think she's very bright (which may stem from herMommy Issues), but proves herself to be the most emotionally mature and inteligent of the group. Plus, she's studying at Worthington Academy, implied to be an Ivy League college.

characters.name: Gail Leery
characters.description: By Season 3, Gail and Mitch make amends (and remarry), and they open together a seafood restaurant,Leery's Fresh Fish, where every main character either works or eats at some point of the series ─quitting then her job as a resident journalist. She becomes pregnant in Season 4 and gives birth to Dawson's (almost a generation younger) sister, Lily. Gail was a main character for the first four seasons of the show, and recurring for the last two. Demoted to Extra: When the characters leave home and head off to university in Season 5, she is removed from the opening credits; in compensation, she gets star billing in the Guest Star credits when she appears. Good Parents: She's a caring mother to Dawson. Depending on the Writer: Her first name's spelling, apparently (Gaile? Gayle? Gayl?). The consensus seems to have settled for 'Gail'.

characters.name: Mitch Leery
characters.description: He's implied to be some kind of office worker at the start of the show, thoughthat isn't very consistent, and he's however mostly seen at house; his motivation to open a restaurant was equally an inconsistent plot thread in the early episodes ─by the thrid season, all of that was abandoned and he became a History teacher and football coach at Dawson's High School, and Gail was the one who opened a seafood restaurant (though in partnership with him). Mitch was a main character for the first four seasons of the show, but was demoted to guest on Season 5 (coinciding with the move to college of the show) and absent on the final season. Back for the Dead: When the characters leave home and head off to university in Season 5, he is removed from the opening credits. The next time he appears,he's killed off. Good Parents: He was a caring father to Dawson, who was shocked byhis death. Only Sane Man: Not really apparent at the start of the show, but he's one of the most mature and emotionally stable characters of the cast.

characters.name: Evelyn "Grams" Ryan
characters.description: Jen's grandmother and the Leery's next-door neighbor, she takes in her granddaughter at the start of the show, and is tending Gramps Ryan, her husband. A former nurse and really Christian woman, her clash with Jen's, who is not a believer, is one of the main plots of Season 1; he would become awidowstarting on Season 2, and shifted to being more of a voice of reason for Jen. By the end of the season, she would take a recently evicted Jack (by his father), in her house, showing her mellowing with Jen and her friends. By Season 4, she's become aReasonable Authority Figurefor all the gang, and starts adoomedrelationship with Mr. Brooks. As a sign of how her relationship with Jen has improved (and how much of a liking she's taken to Jack) she sells her Capeside home at the end of Season 4 in order for her granddaughter to attend Boston University ─and in Season 5, moves there with both Jack and Jen. Grams has the dubious honor of being the only adult character to stay a main character throughout the entirety show. Characterization Marches On: It's revealed midway through Season 5 that she's dating an Afroamerican man, Clifton Smalls (played by Afemo Omilami). Averyfar cry from S1's Gram, who frowned upon Bessie'sMaligned Mixed Marriage. Cool Old Lady: Even Jen, who is certainly her polar opposite, comes to truly respect her.

characters.name: Bess "Bessie" Potter
characters.description: After The Ice House burns down on the Season 2 finale, she struggles to make ends meet until Pacey suggest her and Joey open a B&B on their riverside home ─and helps them starting out. From Season 3 on, she would be the administrator/maid ofPotter's B&B. Bessie would be billed as a main character during the show's first four seasons, and a recurring one for the last two. Advertised Extra: Though she received star billing for four seasons, she appears in less than half of those episodes (this is true of all the parental characters, but Bessie gets hit by it the worst). Demoted to Extra: When the characters leave home and head off to university in Season 5, she is removed from the opening credits; in compensation, she gets star billing in the Guest Star credits when she appears. Feminine Women Can Cook: For the first two seasons, her and Joey's primary income (and source of employment) is a restaurant called the Ice House. When it burns down at the end of the second season, they convert their home into a bed & breakfast.

characters.name: Tamara Jacobs
characters.description: Capeside's High School new English Literature teacher, Tamara enters the show seemingly flirting with Pacey (even though she's nearly 20 years older than him), and, funnily, they both learn later she's her teacher. They, however, initiate a secret relationship that would last until the rumor gets out over the town and she decides to leave Capeside, ending her and Pacey's relationship. She would come back on Season 2 for a couple episodes, where she sells a warehouse of her property to Mitch and gives closure to Pacey. Ambiguous Situation: How much she's serious about Pacey and hers' relationship. For much of Season 1, it's implied she sees it as a legitimate relationship; but she leaves him when there's the risk of people knowing about it. Of course, there's also the matter of their relationshipbeing illegal, which cleary puts her as a sexual predator and taking advantage of Pacey; the show never adresses this ─maybe to keep the situation, well, ambigous. The Bus Came Back: Briefly, for a couple of episodes in Season 2. She gives some much needed resolution and confidence to Pacey and almost gets in withMitch. But she doesn't last too long and skips town. Expy: OfSummer of '42's Dorothy, at least in appearance. This beingDawson's Creek, it gets instantly noted by the main characters. Mrs. Robinson: Of course. Her main purpose on the show is to give Pacey his first romanticand sexualexperience.

characters.name: Bodie Wells
characters.description: A Day in the Limelight: More or less. When the show tackles racism on the Season 3 episodeTo Green With Love, he joins the conversation giving the character his point of view as one of the few black men in a small New England town. Advertised Extra: Always billed as a guest star and an important part of the plot (the brother-in-law of a main character). However, he always pops up for a single scene or too, and almost playing a background character. Commuting on a Bus: He didn't appear on Season 2 and most of Season 3, although Joey was living with her sister and they both rebuilt the house in order to establish a B&B. He was eventually mentioned to be working as a cook elsewhere. The Other Darrin: He was played by a different actor on the Pilot, George Gaffney. A normal viewer wouldn't notice, though —he's roughly a couple of lines there.

characters.name: Abby Morgan
characters.description: Capeside's resident mean girl, she starts on an episodic role in Season 1 and both befriends and antagonizes (normally at the same time) Dawson and his friends. She would become a more relevant figure on Season 2, when she becomes friends to Jen though, as usual, that doesn't last too long. Alpha Bitch: Subverted. She acts like a stereotypicalAlpha Bitchexcept that she isn't popular at all and has no friends whatsoever aside from her on-again, off-again friendship with Jen. Asshole Victim: Literally no one can stand her. Not even her friend Jen was really saddened by her death. Brutal Honesty: She's extremely blunt and rude to people. Deadpan Snarker: Probably the biggest one on the show. She never misses a chance to snark and isnotsubtle about it.

characters.name: Doug Witter
characters.description: Aloof Big Brother: To Pacey. Ambiguously Gay: Played with until the end of the series. By-the-Book Cop: Part of the tension between Doug and Pacey, as Doug is not generally inclined to bend the rules or put up with teenage misbehavior. Coming-Out Story: The series finale deals with this, as Doug finally comes out of the closet rather than lose Jack. Foolish Sibling, Responsible Sibling: The Responsible (a police officer) to Pacey's Foolish.

characters.name: Eve Whitman
characters.description: A mysterious woman who befriends ─or rather, seduces─, Dawson on his bus back to Capeside from Philadelphia at the start of Season 3. She's revealed to be an stripper at the local strip place, and becomes oddly smitten with Dawson. Although at first she seems obsessed to get in his pants, over the course of the first half of the season she's revealed to be somewhat more profound. Replacement Flat Character: She's what Jen would be if herHidden Depthshadn't been revealed over the course of Seasons 1 and 2. Their personality and looks are really similar, but Eve is somewhat more mature and more sex-crazed. Expy:At first, ofRisky Business's Lana, which of course getsinstantly lampshaded. She's a stripper, not a prostitute, though.As noted above, she also serves a similar function to Season 1 Jen, by giving Dawson experiences outside of his comfort zone. She's also really attractive, seductive and blonde. However, she differs greatly in her approach to sex: while that is a point of contention and torture for Jen, Eve seems to rather enjoy being teasing. Makes sense, consideringthey're half sisters. At first, ofRisky Business's Lana, which of course getsinstantly lampshaded. She's a stripper, not a prostitute, though. As noted above, she also serves a similar function to Season 1 Jen, by giving Dawson experiences outside of his comfort zone. She's also really attractive, seductive and blonde. However, she differs greatly in her approach to sex: while that is a point of contention and torture for Jen, Eve seems to rather enjoy being teasing. Makes sense, consideringthey're half sisters.

characters.name: Henry Parker
characters.description: Age-Gap Romance: With Jen, who is a junior (16) while he is a freshman (14). Love Interest: To Jen in Season 3. Naïve Newcomer: A freshman whose innocence is explicitly a part of Jen's attraction to him. Offscreen Breakup: Has one with Jen in Season 4 after he's sent to a private school. She does at least get a"Dear John" Letter. Put on a Bus: Literally, in this case: the last time we see him, during the season finale, he's on a bus with the Capeside football team, heading to summer camp.

characters.name: Principal Howard Green & Nikki Green
characters.description: Nikki Green is a high-schooler who meets Dawson on his visit to Boston University ─an aspiring cinematographer, like him, that nonetheless has a grater success with her submission to the film festival of the university. Her film makes Dawson rethink his aspiration on life and they begin a friendship of sorts. Then she reveals she's Principal Green's daughter and joins the cast at Capeside. Although very important during their short tenure, they both leave the show mid-season, on Episode 3x16. Reasonable Authority Figure: Principal Green is this to the main group. He's aloof at first but understanding of their problems ─a defining moment of the character is when he lets Andie get off being punished forcheating on the PSAT. Small Role, Big Impact: Applies to both of them, but specially to Nikki, who's the first cinema-minded person of his age for Dawson and makes him start searching for his real purpouse in life ─an in film.

characters.name: Arthur 'AJ' Moller Jr.
characters.description: Belligerent Sexual Tension: With Joey,obviously. They start arguing on their very first scene together, and only make amends once he shows his tender and vulnerable side. Subverted, however, since once they start dating they are soft with each other -and they'renot meant to beanyways. Derailing Love Interests: Of course. His main purpouse isnotgettingPaceyand Joey together ─and making her keep believing she still doesn't feel anything for herfriend. Foil: ToPacey, of course. He's cultured, very mature (even condescending), and older than Joey ─or, in other words, the contrary of his foil. Physically, however, they lookverymuch alike. Gender-Blender Name: Which makes for a really funny scene where both Joey and him believe they are to meet a person of their same gender for the tour of the university. Mirror Character: He's this for Dawson, as Morgan is for Joey. An idealistic, culturally-minded and terriblyOblivious to Loveyoung man who's too intelligent for his own good, just as Dawson. Morgan is an artsy-minded young woman, shy but really sensitive ─just as Joey. Their story also makes aPlot Parallelto both Joey and Dawson's story and Joey andPacey'sby the point he shows up on the show.

characters.name: Ethan Brady
characters.description: Master of the Mixed Message:Every single oneof his appearences is like this. Jack even reiterates he feels something for Ethan, to no avail; Ethan insists on keeping their relationship platonic since he's "teaching" Jack how to develop as a gay man. His last appearences, however, prove he liked Jack all along. He dares Jack to kiss him in public, but Jack is too scared to do it, although he reciprocicates. On the S3 finale, Jack thoughens upand kisses him, to no avail, however, since Ethan had already gotten back with his ex-boyfriend. Small Role, Big Impact: As it happens with a lot of Season 3 characters. He's Jack's first gay friend and semi-boyfriend, although he only stars in five episodes. Straight Gay: The only hint one would have about his orientation are his own words. And, of course, theBig Damn Kisshe andJackshare on the finale of his debuting and only season.

characters.name: Gretchen Witter
characters.description: Age-Gap Romance: With Dawson, who is a senior (17) while she is in college (20). All Girls Want Bad Boys: A part of herDark and Troubled Past. Characterization Marches On: When she first appears, she has kind of a mean-spirited streak. That disappears after some episodes, and she's generally very sweet natured. Childhood Friend Romance: With Dawson. She was the first girl he ever had a crush on. Cool Big Sis: To Pacey. And all along the season she stars in, she gradually becomes one to all the gang.

characters.name: Drue Valentine
characters.description: The son of the owner of Capeside's yatch club, who becomes a recurring character for all of Season 4 when Joey starts working there as a waitress. Turns out he was a part of Jen's circle back when she lived in New York, before the start of the show. A spoilt rich kid, he fulfills the role of Capeside's ─and Dawson's circle─ resident villain. Amicable Exes: With Jen, more or less. They had some kind of a relationship back in New York, and rekindle their friendship in Capeside ─eventually. Alpha Bitch: A male version. He exists to cuase trouble to the gang ─but specially to Jen. Belligerent Sexual Tension: With Jen, since he reminds her of her past in New York (and, to be fair, it's pretty unsufferable). Surprisingly, with Joey too. Expy: A pretty obvious male (and rich) version of Abby Morgan, which of course getslampshaded:Gretchen:Who's him?Dawson:Did you know Abby Morgan?

characters.name: Mr. Brooks
characters.description: A memeber of Capeside's yatch club whose boat Dawson steals (technically), and whom Dawson has to work for in order to repay him. He's a rich man who lives alone and constantly critizes Dawson and his friends ─until it turns out they're not that different. Grumpy Old Man: Introduced as "Albert Brooks," a persnickety customer at the Yacht Club, whom Dawson steals a boat from (it's a long story). Hidden Depths: ...who turns out to be "A. I. Brooks," a famous film director from the black-and-white era. He and Dawson collaborate on a memoir documentary about his (Brooks') career, which helps boost Dawson's reputation as a filmmaker. History Repeats: Left the film industry after his best (male) friend and his girlfriend fell in love under his nose. Did we mention that this is the season when Pacey and Joey are dating? Lonely Funeral: Exactly five people show up: Dawson, Gale, Mitch, Gretchen and Grams.Allof them met him because Dawson stole his boat, leading Dawson to bleakly speculate what might have happened if he had done no such thing.

characters.name: Tobey Barrett
characters.description: A member of the local Gay-Straight alliance. Jen and Jack met him when she drags his friend to a reunion. He's initially dismissive of howstraightJack tries to act ─but is harboring, in fact, a secret crush on him. He later invites Jack to a program for helping children read, where they progressively get closer until Jen (on Jack's behalf) invites him to their graduation. He becomes Jack's first official boyfriend. Toby is one of the few seasonal characters that survive until the following Season (the only notable others being Abby Morgan and Todd Carr). Camp Gay: In a show where all gay men were prettynon-flamboyant, he's the most camp it could get. His (in-universe)campinesscomes, however, from how open he is about his sexuality and how much of an activist he is. In real life, he'd probably be consideredStraight Gay. Queer Character, Queer Actor: He may be the only gay man on the show actually portrayed by an actor of the same orientation. Jerkass Has a Point: Although veeery debatable, but later seasons (5 specifically) prove he has a point by instantly regarding Jack as someone who's not really comfortable with his identity. You could argue too that his initial belligerence towards Jack may be a way of masquerading his crush on him, too.The show doesn't really get that much on the conversation, but he has a very valid point (though expressed in a very forceful way) in that his Gay-Straight alliance wants to offer a safe and free space for people who are gay or bisexual, since he geats beaten up just for being on the street near the S4 finale.

characters.name: Todd Carr
characters.description: A film director and Dawson's tutor during his internship as a Freshman in college ─which lasts exactly a single day because of how abrasive and demanding to his workers he is. Although Dawson leaves and goes back to Capeside, Todd doesn't forget him, and he reappers on Season's 5 finale,still being a jerkbut wanting to hire Dawson as his assitant. During Season 6, Todd becomes a more central part of the cast and develops anOdd Friendshipof sorts with Dawson. Intergenerational Friendship: Ozsan is actually just a year older than James Van Der Beek, but the character is implied to be at least ten years older than Dawson and has been married when he enters the show. Plus, he also acts as a mentor to Dawson in the film industry. Vitriolic Best Buds: He becomes an unexpected best friend and confidante to Dawson ─although they barely stand being in the same room. He actually shares more scenes with him in Season 6 than Pacey,his alleged best friend forever.

characters.name: Charlie Todd
characters.description: Jen's boyfriend duringthe startof Season 5. He's a musician (he plays the guitar and sings at a rock band) the gang meet at a Homecoming party, and apparently very sensitive and a music geek. That's nothing more than a façade; he's actually a player who tries to bed several women ─includingJoey. Ladykiller in Love: For the first half of the season, he's a smarmy ladykiller, who has no trouble dating both Jen and another woman without asking they first about such an arrangement; plus, he also lies a lot about himself and his personality in order to seduce anything that has a skirt. In the second half of the season, however, he suddenly falls head-over-heels for Joey, and goes to great lengths to be with her (he seems to forget how girl-crazed he was). In fact, it's her who breaks up with him, and as a response, he... serenades her.Screw you, Jen! Mr. Fanservice: Not only for the viewers ─characters in-universe can't seem to shut up about how much of a sexy body he has. Satellite Love Interest: He's not this initially, since he's meant to be a newfoe/friendto the gang ─in fact, he gets involved in several activities during his debuting and only season. It's only during his relationship with Joey that he becomes this, where he just serves the purpouse of showing how much of a wonderful and perfect it-girl she is that she turns around men's lives. Suspiciously Similar Substitute: Well, in theory he came to replace Drue's spot as a frenemy to the gang (and Jen in particular) but this trope is averted, as he only fills thatrole; his actual personality differs a lot from Drue's, the only thing in common being that both are very charismatic.

characters.name: Eddie Doling
characters.description: Joey's boyfriend formostof Season 6. He starts off as Joey's brooding and mysterious Literature colleage, and bothend up working at the same bar Emma tends. It turns out he's an aspiring writer who was attending University's classes while not being enrolled; after a very, verytortuosrelationship with Joey, he ends up leaving with an scolarship for Los Angeles. Bitch in Sheep's Clothing: His last episode confirms him as one. On it, Eddie invites Joey to a trip to Europe for 5$ a destiny. However, this leads to an argument between them, since she starts getting overwhelmed.Right afterwards, she leaves Joey with a mere note.So much for the love of Joey's life! Derailing Love Interests: His main porpouse is to keep Joey from Pacey during Season 6. The Masochism Tango: It seems that, the more time Joey and Eddie date, the more they hate each other. Satellite Love Interest: Another one of Joey's long, long list of boyfriends who consider themselves "too inferior" to how good and marvellous of a girl she is. To be fair, he has more development than previous love interests; but looking at his screentime, he almost exclusively only has interactions with Joey, or on scenes where Joey is the POV character.

characters.name: Natasha Kelly
characters.description: Dawson's love interest for most of Season 6. She (or rather, Dawson's relationship with her) is the responsible for him and Joey's fight-of-the-season that keeps them apart for most of it. Natasha is an experienced (although implied to be not very good) actress hired for Todd's slasher movie whose filming takes up most of the season. A very outspoken and free lady, she's unlike any woman Dawson has dated before. Actor-Shared Background: Maybe. Kajilich is from the US, but of Slavik ascent on her father's side. The show never indulges in her background, but Natasha is a name of Slavik origin. Aloof Dark-Haired Girl: Given Dawson's still an innocentWide-Eyed Idealisteven on the last season of the show, she's more experienced and mature both in film and in life than him by comparison. She's very tall too (almost as tall as Dawson) and has long, dark hair, combined with an aloof and cynical personality. She's more sociable and party-loving than most examples, though. Hard-Drinking Party Girl: Another point of difference between her and Dawson. A downplayed example, though, since she doesn't have any of the negative traits associated with this trope. Pair the Spares: Given the ending of the show, most fans interpretDawsonand Natasha end up marrying, since they end their relationship on friendly terms.
